1	\chapter{Coherence of locally trivial line bundles}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundleCoherence.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter supplies the cheap, on-path half of the
6	% line-bundle coherence requirement of the A.2.c Quot-scheme embedding
7	% (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}).
8	% The Quot functor takes coherent quotients of a coherent sheaf; to feed it a
9	% line bundle one must know that the line bundle is itself coherent, i.e. a
10	% finitely presented \(\mathcal{O}\)-module. The embedding's input is a point of
11	% the relative Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), whose carrier is the
12	% \emph{locally trivial} predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) of
13	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (the project-side stand-in for an
14	% invertible sheaf, a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules locally isomorphic to
15	% \(\mathcal{O}\) of rank one). The chapter proves
16	% \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial} M \Rightarrow M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\),
17	% together with a rank-one / flatness record. The general implication
18	% ``\(M\) tensor-invertible \(\Rightarrow\) \(M\) locally free of rank one''
19	% (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma~0B8M when stalks are local) is
20	% \emph{not} crossed here: the carrier is already locally trivial, so the
21	% finite-presentation spreading-out is never needed.
22	
23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:lbc_setup}
25	
26	Fix a scheme \(X\) and write \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\) for the category of sheaves
27	of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) on the
28	ringed-space site of \(X\), with structure sheaf \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)).
29	A \emph{line bundle} on \(X\) is an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module; in the
30	sense of \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} we model line bundles by the
31	predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\): a module \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) is locally
32	trivial of rank one if every point \(x \in X\) has an \emph{affine} open
33	neighbourhood \(U\) on which the restriction \(M|_U\) is isomorphic, as an
34	\(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, to the structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U}\):
35	\[
36	  \texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M) \;:=\;
37	  \forall x \in X,\ \exists\, U \ni x \text{ affine open},\quad
38	  M|_U \;\cong\; \struct{U}.
39	\]
40	This is the rank-one case of Stacks Definition~17.25.1
41	(Tag~01CS, invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules), and
42	agrees with the classical ``locally free of rank one'' description by Stacks
43	Lemma~17.25.4 (Tag~0B8M).
44	
45	The goal of the chapter is to record that such a module is \emph{coherent} in the
46	sense the Quot construction consumes, namely finitely presented:
47	\(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) in Mathlib's
48	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) (built from a
49	\(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\)). The mathematics is elementary: on each
50	trivialising chart \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) is finite free of rank one, hence
51	has the trivial finite presentation, and finite presentation is by definition a
52	local condition. No stalk computation and no spreading-out are involved. The
53	content is the bookkeeping that turns the pointwise trivialisation into the
54	indexed \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) Mathlib expects.
55	
56	\section{Site instances for quasi-coherent data}
57	\label{sec:lbc_site_instances}
58	
59	% NOTE (chief unknown). Mathlib's SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation /
60	% QuasicoherentData are stated under three site hypotheses on the slice
61	% topologies J.over X of the underlying site J of X.ringCatSheaf:
62	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat)]
63	%   [∀ x, HasWeakSheafify (J.over x) AddCommGrpCat]
64	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat]
65	% These must be discharged (or assumed as instances) before the statements in
66	% this chapter typecheck for X.ringCatSheaf. The project already lives in the
67	% SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf world, so these should resolve; this is the one
68	% item to verify when the Lean file is scaffolded.
69	
70	The Mathlib predicates \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) and
71	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\) are stated relative to a
72	Grothendieck topology \(J\) on a base category \(C\) and a sheaf of rings
73	\(R : \Sheaf(J, \RingCat)\). They require, for every object \(x : C\), three
74	instances on the slice topology \(J/x\) (Mathlib \(J.\mathtt{over}\ x\)):
75	\[
76	  (J/x).\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}(\forget_{\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat}),\quad
77	  \HasWeakSheafify(J/x, \AddCommGrpCat),\quad
78	  (J/x).\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}(\AddCommGrpCat).
79	\]
80	For \(X.\mathtt{Modules} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\ X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
81	these are the ambient site instances of the ringed space underlying \(X\). We
82	record their availability as a standing assumption for the chapter; verifying it
83	for \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is the single instance-resolution check the
84	formalisation must clear at entry, and is independent of the mathematical
85	content below.
86	
87	\begin{remark}
88	  \label{rem:lbc_site_instances}
89	  For the ringed-space site underlying \(X\) with sheaf of rings
90	  \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), every slice topology \(J/x\) carries the
91	  three instances \(\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}\), \(\HasWeakSheafify\) and
92	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) (for the forgetful functor
93	  \(\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat\)) demanded by
94	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\). Under this assumption the
95	  predicate \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) is well-formed for every
96	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\).
97	\end{remark}
98	
99	\section{From pointwise triviality to a trivialising cover}
100	\label{sec:lbc_cover}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}\leanok
103	[Trivialising affine cover]
104	  \label{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover}
106	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
107	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Definition 17.25.1
108	  % "invertible module" (Tag 01CS), Section 17.25 (Tag 01CR)
109	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4059).
110	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
111	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
112	  %  {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
113	  %  of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
114	  %  the functor
115	  %  $$
116	  %  \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
117	  %  \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
118	  %  $$
119	  %  is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
120	  %  {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
121	  %  to $\mathcal{O}_X$."
122	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CS (Modules, Definition~17.25.1).}
123	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\).
124	  Then there exist an index set \(I\), a family of affine open subschemes
125	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(X\) covering \(X\) (i.e. \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\)), and
126	  for each \(i\) an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
127	  \[
128	    e_i \;:\; M|_{U_i} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \struct{U_i}
129	  \]
130	  to the structure sheaf-as-module on \(U_i\).
131	\end{lemma}
132	
133	\begin{proof}
134	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
135	  \leanok
136	  Apply the hypothesis \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\) at every point \(x \in X\): it
137	  produces an affine open \(U_x \ni x\) and an isomorphism
138	  \(e_x : M|_{U_x} \cong \struct{U_x}\). Take the index set \(I := X\) (the
139	  underlying set of points), and to the index \(x\) assign the chosen
140	  neighbourhood \(U_x\) and the chosen isomorphism \(e_x\). The family
141	  \(\{U_x\}_{x \in X}\) covers \(X\) because each \(x\) lies in its own \(U_x\).
142	  This is exactly the data the definition supplies, packaged as an indexed
143	  family rather than a pointwise existential.
144	\end{proof}
145	
146	\section{The trivial presentation on a chart}
147	\label{sec:lbc_chart_presentation}
148	
149	\begin{lemma}\leanok
150	[Finite free presentation on a chart]
151	  \label{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation}
153	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
154	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
155	  % "Modules of finite presentation" (\label{definition-finite-presentation})
156	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L1377-L1392).
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
158	  % "We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is of {\it finite presentation}
159	  %  if for every point $x \in X$ there exists an open neighbourhood
160	  %  $x\in U \subset X$, and  $n, m \in \mathbf{N}$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
161	  %  is isomorphic to the cokernel of a map
162	  %  $$
163	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{j = 1, \ldots, m}
164	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
165	  %  \longrightarrow
166	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1, \ldots, n}
167	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
168	  %  $$"
169	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Modules of finite
170	  presentation''.}
171	  On each chart \(U_i\) of the trivialising cover of
172	  \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}, the restriction \(M|_{U_i}\) admits the
173	  trivial finite free presentation
174	  \[
175	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \struct{U_i} \;\longrightarrow\; M|_{U_i}
176	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
177	  \]
178	  i.e. \(M|_{U_i}\) is the cokernel of the zero map
179	  \(\bigoplus_{j \in \varnothing} \struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\)
180	  (the case \(m = 0\), \(n = 1\) of the finite-presentation pattern). The
181	  unit sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i}\) (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\))
182	  carries a canonical free presentation on one generator with no relations;
183	  this presentation is finite.
184	\end{lemma}
185	
186	\begin{proof}
187	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
188	  \leanok
189	  The structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,
190	  (U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is free of rank one on the single generator
191	  \(1\): the identity map \(\struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\) exhibits it as
192	  \(\bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), so the surjection
193	  \(\struct{U_i}^{\oplus 1} \twoheadrightarrow \struct{U_i}\) is an isomorphism
194	  and its kernel is zero, generated by the empty family. Thus \(\struct{U_i}\)
195	  has the canonical presentation with \(n = 1\) generators and \(m = 0\)
196	  relations; this canonical \(\mathtt{unit}\)-presentation is the rank-one free
197	  presentation, and Mathlib records it as \emph{finite} (the
198	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\) finite-presentation instance).
199	
200	  The chart presentation is obtained by transporting the canonical finite

1	\chapter{The sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence (shared slice root)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SheafOverEquivalence}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/SheafOverEquivalence.lean
4	
5	\section{The equivalence}
6	\label{sec:soe_equivalence}
7	
8	\begin{definition}
9	
10	\leanok
11	[Sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence]
12	  \label{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence}
14	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is an Archon-original assembly
15	  % of existing Mathlib primitives, NOT an external literature citation, so it
16	  % carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). The primitive instantiated is
17	  % SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence, defined at
18	  % Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean:305
19	  % (section Equivalence, lines 289--329): "equivalence of SheafOfModules
20	  % categories from a site equivalence e : C \simeq D together with a morphism of
21	  % the two structure ring sheaves and its inverse, with two coherences". The
22	  % underlying site equivalence is TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence
23	  % (Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean:41), and the continuity prerequisites are
24	  % the documented TODO at Over.lean:19--22.
25	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
26	  immersion \(\iota : \widetilde{U} \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing site
27	  equivalence
28	  \[
29	    e \;:=\; \mathtt{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U
30	       \;:\; \mathtt{Over}\,U \;\simeq\; \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}
31	  \]
32	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) (carrying the slice
33	  topology \((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\)) and the
34	  opens of the subspace \(\widetilde{U}\) (carrying
35	  \(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,\widetilde{U}\)) --- lifts to an
36	  \emph{equivalence of sheaf-of-modules categories}
37	  \[
38	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}\;:\;
39	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
40	        \;\;\simeq\;\;
41	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
42	  \]
43	  the modules-level lift of \(e\) that transports the structure ring sheaf. It is
44	  obtained by instantiating
45	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) at \(e\), with the
46	  open-immersion structure-sheaf ring morphism
47	  \[
48	    \varphi \;:\;
49	      X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U
50	      \;\longrightarrow\;
51	      \bigl(e.\mathtt{functor}.\mathtt{sheafPushforwardContinuous}\bigr).\mathrm{obj}\,
52	        \bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
53	  \]
54	  --- sectionwise at an object \(V \in \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the open-immersion
55	  comparison \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left}) \to \mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(e\,V)\),
56	  i.e.\ the section-level isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) of the structure
57	  sheaves --- together with its inverse \(\psi\) and the two coherences
58	  \(H_1, H_2\) expressing that \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are mutually inverse.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	\begin{proof}
62	
63	\leanok
64	  The construction is the assembly of three existing Mathlib primitives; the only
65	  step carrying mathematical content is the ring morphism \(\varphi\).
66	
67	  \emph{Continuity (no work).} The equivalence
68	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) requires both legs of the site
69	  equivalence \(e\) to be continuous:
70	  \(e.\mathtt{functor}\) and \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\). For a dense-subsite inclusion
71	  continuity is automatic --- it is a typeclass instance --- and for an
72	  equivalence the density of one leg propagates to the other. The declaration
73	  \(\mathtt{overEquivInverseIsDenseSubsite}\) exhibits \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\) as a
74	  dense subsite of the slice site, from which both continuity legs follow by
75	  inference. The Mathlib TODO at \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22)
76	  is discharged for this case.
77	
78	  \emph{The ring morphism \(\varphi\) (the genuine content).} The two structure
79	  ring sheaves are different objects on different sites:
80	  \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\), and
81	  \((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\).
82	  The open immersion \(\iota\) identifies them: at an object \(V \in
83	  \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the sections \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left})\) and
84	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(\iota^{-1}(V.\mathrm{left}))\) agree through the
85	  open-immersion section isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\). This is verbatim the
86	  datum that \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor}\) already uses inline
87	  (its structure map is \((\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\,\cdot).\mathrm{inv}\)). Packaging
88	  this section-level isomorphism naturally in \(V\) yields \(\varphi\); its inverse
89	  is \(\psi\), and the coherences \(H_1, H_2\) record that the
90	  \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) round-trips are identities, proved by the
91	  inverse-iso laws (\(\mathtt{Sheaf.hom\_ext}\) reduces them sectionwise).
92	
93	  Feeding \(e\), \(\varphi\), \(\psi\), \(H_1\), \(H_2\) into
94	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) produces the asserted equivalence.
95	  Its underlying functor is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\), which
96	  is what the two consumer isomorphisms below unfold.
97	\end{proof}
98	
99	\section{The equivalence carries restriction and unit}
100	\label{sec:soe_consumers}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}
103	
104	\leanok
105	[The over-equivalence carries restriction to over]
106	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictOverIso}
108	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
109	  Let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Under the over-equivalence of
110	  \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is a canonical isomorphism
111	  \[
112	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,\bigl(M|_\iota\bigr)
113	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
114	    M.\mathtt{over}\,U,
115	  \]
116	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the restriction \(M|_\iota\) of \(M\) along the
117	  open immersion to the slice object \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\).
118	\end{lemma}
119	
120	\begin{proof}
121	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
122	  \leanok
123	  The restriction \(M|_\iota\) is \emph{itself} a pushforward of sheaves of
124	  modules: by construction \(M|_\iota = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\)
125	  applied along \(\iota.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) (the open-immersion functor
126	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U} \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\)). The functor underlying
127	  the over-equivalence is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). Their
128	  composite is again a single pushforward, identified by
129	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp}\), which here is the identity
130	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{Iso.refl}\). It remains to identify the two underlying
131	  index functors \(\mathtt{Over}\,U \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\) entering the two
132	  pushforwards: both send an object \(V\) to its left component \(V.\mathrm{left}\),
133	  so they are equal, and \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatIso}\) along the \(\mathtt{eqToIso}\)
134	  of that equality transports the composite to \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\). The whole
135	  argument mirrors, step for step, the existing
136	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso}\).
137	\end{proof}
138	
139	\begin{lemma}
140	
141	\leanok
142	[The over-equivalence carries unit to unit]
143	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitOverIso}
145	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
146	  Under the over-equivalence of \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is
147	  a canonical isomorphism
148	  \[
149	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,
150	      \bigl(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
151	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
152	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
153	  \]
154	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the unit module (the structure sheaf-as-module) of
155	  the subspace site to the unit module of the slice site.
156	\end{lemma}
157	
158	\begin{proof}
159	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
160	  \leanok
161	  The functor underlying the over-equivalence is
162	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). The pushforward of a unit module
163	  along a morphism of ringed sites is the unit module of the target ring sheaf,
164	  transported along the ring identification \(\varphi\): the structure sheaf
165	  pushes forward to the structure sheaf because \(\varphi\) is precisely the
166	  open-immersion identification of the two structure ring sheaves. Concretely the
167	  pushforward-of-unit computation produces \(\mathtt{unit}\) of the codomain ring
168	  sheaf up to \(\varphi\), and \(\varphi\) being an isomorphism makes this a
169	  genuine isomorphism.
170	
171	  Concretely, the canonical comparison map realising this transport is the
172	  unit-to-pushforward-of-unit morphism
173	  \[
174	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi
175	      \;:\;
176	      \mathtt{unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)
177	      \;\longrightarrow\;
178	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,
179	        \bigl(\mathtt{unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr).
180	  \]
181	  Its section at an open \(W\) is exactly the section-level ring map
182	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\): the morphism acts on a section by multiplication
183	  through \(\varphi\), so on \(W\) it is the additive-group map underlying
184	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\) (the characterisation
185	  \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit\_val\_app\_apply}\)). Since \(\varphi\) is an
186	  isomorphism of ring sheaves, each component \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\) is a ring
187	  isomorphism, hence its underlying additive-group map is an isomorphism.
188	
189	  It follows that \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
190	  One reflects the sectionwise isomorphism upward through the two forgetful
191	  functors --- first \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.forget}\) (down to presheaves of
192	  modules), then \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf}\) (down to presheaves of
193	  additive groups) --- so that being an isomorphism reduces to the componentwise
194	  isomorphism check
195	  \(\mathtt{NatTrans.isIso\_iff\_isIso\_app}\) on the underlying natural
196	  transformation, which is the sectionwise statement just established. The desired
197	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{unitOverIso}\) is then the inverse of the resulting
198	  isomorphism, \((\mathtt{asIso}\,(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi))^{-1}\).
199	  This is the slice-site analogue of
200	  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnitIso}\).

1	\chapter{Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-173). This chapter is the A.1.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the line-bundle
7	% pullback functor \(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) along the
8	% projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) of a relative curve, and the
9	% set-valued relative Picard presheaf
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) := \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\)
11	% as a functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). This chapter is gated on
12	% \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (iter-172) for the relative-spectrum machinery
13	% that any concrete Lean-side affine-cover argument will need, and it itself
14	% gates A.1.c (\'etale sheafification / \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a sheaf), A.2.*
15	% (Quot / Hilbert representability), A.3 (identity component), and A.4
16	% (universal property = Albanese / Jacobian).
17	
18	% NOTE (planner directive iter-173). Per the iter-173 blueprint-review
19	% recommendation (see .archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-route173.md,
20	% sub-phase choice), this chapter defines \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a
21	% \emph{set-valued} presheaf on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). The abelian-group refinement
22	% (and the \'etale-sheafification step) belong to the follow-up chapter
23	% Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex (A.1.c). The set-valued formulation lets us discharge
24	% well-definedness of the quotient and functoriality of \(g^*\) as soon as a
25	% line-bundle pullback functor and its composition identity are in hand, with
26	% no reliance on Mathlib's \texttt{AddCommGrp} \'etale-sheafification API which
27	% does not yet exist.
28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an
78	  open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(C \times_k T\) on which each restriction
79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}
87

89	% as `Type (u+1) := sorry` (a TYPE-level scaffold sorry), mirroring the
90	% iter-173 precedent for `Scheme.QcohAlgebra`. Mathlib commit `b80f227`
91	% ships no `IsInvertible` predicate on `Scheme.Modules`; the closest is
92	% `Module.Invertible` (rings-only, in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`).
93	% iter-175+ refines the Lean body to a `structure` pairing a
94	% `(pullback πC πT).Modules` carrier with an invertibility witness (either
95	% Mathlib upstream of an `IsInvertible`-on-sheaf-of-modules predicate, or
96	% a project-side cover-by-affine-opens reduction to `Module.Invertible R`
97	% over each chart).
98	%

100	% `@[reducible] noncomputable def OnProduct := (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules`

1	\chapter{The relative Picard functor and its \'etale sheafification}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.1.c sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It upgrades the set-valued
7	% relative Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
8	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b) to its abelian-group-valued
9	% refinement and then to its \'etale sheafification
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\). The chapter is gated on
11	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b, pullback functor + set-valued
12	% quotient on disk) and on \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, the
13	% relative-spectrum machinery underlying any concrete Lean-side computation).
14	% It \emph{gates} A.2.c (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) since
15	% Grothendieck's existence theorem represents precisely the \'etale-sheafified
16	% functor (cf.\ [Kleiman], \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
17	% \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).''). It is \emph{not} responsible for representability
18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
19	% (that is A.3).
20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the
23	% quotient \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) that the set-valued chapter
24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup
26	% because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,
27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf
28	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) in the \'etale topology; the
29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf
30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, \(\Pic_X\) is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below \(\Pic_X\) is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},
33	% which is the right notion for Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman representability.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application},
10	% \texttt{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}) (read from
11	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
16	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
17	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
18	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
19	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
20	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
21	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
22	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
23	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
24	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
25	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
26	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
27	
28	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
29	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
30	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
31	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
32	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
33	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
34	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
35	\[
36	  \begin{array}{ccc}
37	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
38	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
39	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
40	  \end{array}
41	\]
42	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
43	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
44	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
45	
46	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
47	
48	\begin{definition}
49	
50	\leanok
51	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
52	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
53	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
54	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, lem:push_pull_functor}
55	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
56	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
57	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
58	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
59	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
60	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
61	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
62	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
63	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
64	  % the unit ideal, see
65	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
66	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
67	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
68	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
69	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
70	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
71	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
72	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
73	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
74	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
75	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
76	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
77	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
78	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
79	  \[
80	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
81	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
82	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
83	  \]
84	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
85	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
86	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
87	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
88	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
89	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
90	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
91	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
92	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
93	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
94	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
95	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
96	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
97	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
98	\end{definition}
99	
100	\begin{definition}

1	\chapter{FGA representability of the Picard scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean
4

16	% material (\Picz) is A.3 and lives in a separate sibling chapter --- it is
17	% \emph{not} scoped into this chapter.
18	
19	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). This chapter must produce the
20	% \emph{representable Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) as a \(k\)-group scheme,
21	% locally of finite type, that represents the \'etale sheafification
22	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) defined in A.1.c. The three deliverables are
23	% (i) a line-bundle / Quot translation expressed via the Abel map
24	% \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (Kleiman \S 3, \S 4) which factorises through the

26	% Quot engine of A.2.b; (ii) the existence theorem itself, in the
27	% field-of-definition specialisation \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper
28	% geometrically integral curve; (iii) the group-scheme refinement carried by
29	% the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The Lean
30	% encoding is delegated to a Lean file
31	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean} whose
32	% construction is described in the final section but not written in this
33	% chapter.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).

1	\chapter{Relative Spec}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).

8	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) -- a projective \(S\)-scheme representing the

23	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
24	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
46	
47	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1 and
48	% \S 5; FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
49	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500 +
50	% L2154-L2542). The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
51	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned

53	% the verbatim source-quote in \ is taken from
54	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
55	% reference).

1	\chapter{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-184). This chapter is the A.3 sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It builds the
7	% identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of the Picard scheme as an
8	% open-and-closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\), equips it with the
9	% degree-zero characterisation, and identifies it as an abelian variety
10	% of dimension \(g(C)\). The abstract identity-component substrate
11	% (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme} +
12	% \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is new project material:
13	% the Lean target
14	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} does not yet
15	% exist in Mathlib.
16	%
17	% NOTE: future split candidate when a second consumer materialises. The
18	% abstract identity-component substrate is kept in this chapter (rather
19	% than split off into a separate
20	% \texttt{GroupScheme\_IdentityComponent.tex}) because it is small and
21	% has a single consumer for now. If a second consumer materialises,
22	% revisit the split.
23	
24	% NOTE (planner directive iter-184). The Lean target file
25	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean} does not yet
26	% exist; this chapter is the authoritative specification, and the Lean
27	% skeleton is owed in a follow-up iteration.
28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.
78	  %
79	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
80	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
81	  % extending \(k\).
82	  %  \end{lem}"
83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}

=== AbelJacobi.tex ===
68:  \emph{Classical description.} The classical proof of the same statement proceeds in two parts. In positive genus (\(g(C) > 0\)), the existence and uniqueness of the factorisation come from the Pic-scheme representability theorem together with the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism: see Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, Route~A (sub-steps A.1--A.4) for the line-bundle / universal-property route via \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), and Route~B (sub-steps B.1--B.3) for the symmetric-power / Stein-factorisation route. In genus \(0\), the same statement reduces to the rigidity theorem for \(C \to A\) --- the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}. This rigidity result is established \emph{over the algebraic closure \(\bar k\)}: its signature carries an \(\mathrm{IsAlgClosed}\,\bar k\) hypothesis, and it is built out of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) over the algebraically closed base. The resulting equality of morphisms is then descended back to the original base field \(k\) by faithfully-flat descent: because \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\) is faithfully flat, two \(k\)-morphisms that agree after base change to \(\bar k\) already agree over \(k\) (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}). See Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, sub-cases C.1--C.3, in particular the seven-step expansion C.2.a--C.2.g, where sub-step C.2.f (faithfully-flat descent of morphism equality from \(\bar k\) back to \(k\)) is the descent step carrying the algebraically-closed-base rigidity conclusion down to the original base field; a base-change-to-\(\bar k\) step (establishing rigidity) followed by this descent step is on the M2 critical path. The protected signature of Theorem~REF does \emph{not} assume \(C(k) \neq \emptyset\), so the genus-\(0\) argument branches on whether a \(k\)-rational point exists. When \(C(k) \neq \emptyset\), the supplied \(P \in C(k)\) feeds the pointing hypothesis of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} directly --- no \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_k\) identification is required, sidestepping the Brauer--Severi obstruction (Brauer--Severi conics over \(\mathbb Q\) are the standard counterexample to an unconditional \(k\)-side identification). When \(C(k) = \emptyset\), the \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\) field of the genus-\(0\) witness (\cref{def:genusZeroWitness}) is vacuously true at the Lean type level: the universal quantifier \(\forall\, (P \colon \mathbf{1} \to C)\) ranges over the empty type. None of this classical content is replayed in the Lean formalisation: it is bundled into the deferred existence hypothesis Theorem~REF, whose proof is the project's single explicit foundational gap.
=== Jacobian.tex ===
450:    \item Sub-step C.2 reduces, via the project's already-formalised rigidity lemma (Theorem~REF of Chapter~REF), to the classical fact that proper rational curves on a positive-dimensional smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) are constant (the keystone of Sub-step~C.2.d, packaged characteristic-free as \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}; the over-\(\bar k\) \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} is the retained fallback-(a) artifact). This classical fact is not in Mathlib in the form needed here; see Sub-step~C.2.g for the explicit gap statement and the committed route~(c) chain. The descent of morphism equality from \(\bar k\) back to the base field \(k\) (Sub-step~C.2.f) has a short \emph{final} epi-cancellation (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}) but is, as a whole, a multi-iteration base-change sub-build rather than a \(\sim 2\)-line step (see the re-costing at C.2.f).
598:  \item \textbf{Layer I --- direct definition.} The Jacobian of \(C\) is defined uniformly as the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness provided by Theorem~REF, carrying the four properties of Theorems~REF--REF (each obtained by projection from the witness). The genus-\(0\) case is included: the witness object is the trivial \(\Spec k\), and the witness's universal property carries only the rigidity \emph{statement} for morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-\(0\) curve to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme --- \textbf{decoupled} from the Picard representability of Route~A. This rigidity is established over the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\) via the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of the upstream \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (carrying \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\), no \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\)), whose proof route is the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (cube-free) of that chapter (route~(c)); the over-\(\bar k\) \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} is the retained fallback-(a) artifact (the differential / Serre-duality route, shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii)). The conclusion over a general base field \(k\) is then obtained by \emph{descending} the over-\(\bar k\) morphism equality along the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\) (whose closing epi-cancellation is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}; the surrounding base change is a multi-iteration sub-build, see Sub-step~C.2.f). The genus-\(0\) arm is packaged as the explicit Lean construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}: the underlying scheme is the terminal object \(\Spec k\), the universal morphism in the \(C(k) \neq \emptyset\) branch is the unique terminal map \texttt{toUnit}\,\(C\), and the universal factorisation reduces to \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} over \(\bar k\) followed by descent to \(k\).
